body_txt: In Real Life , the trunks of cars are mainly used for long-term storage of road safety gear and spare tires, and short-term storage of bulky items that take up too much space in the cab of the vehicle, like groceries or golf clubs. In fiction, though, especially Crime Fiction , Spy Fiction , or Horror , trunks tend to be used for storing people, alive or dead. This can be for several reasons:  The last one overlaps with Dead Man's Chest , and may be part of a scheme for Disposing of a Body . A fair amount of Artistic Licence applies to this trope today. For cars sold in the United States of America, Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standard # 401 requires glow-in-the-dark release catches inside the trunks of cars manufactured from the 2002 model year and beyond, and trunk release levers are common in virtually all modern cars regardless of where they are sold. So overall, the ability to trap alive and conscious people in cars today is significantly less plausible than in years past. Note that it's only a "trunk" in countries which use American English. The rest of the English speaking world calls it a "boot" (The term boot originally comes from stagecoaches, originally referring to side seats but later a storage area in the coach ). Compare Vehicular Kidnapping , and Creepy Stalker Van . See also Trunk Shot .
